coredata.dc:title: Analysis of thermal runaway propagation behavior and suppression in overcharged ternary lithium-ion battery modules
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Lingrui, Kong
Hao, Liu
Tianwei, Zhang
Jikai, Tian
Gengyi, Su
Xiaorui, Zhang
coredata.dc:description: Thermal runaway (TR) caused by overcharging of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) often leads to fires in energy storage systems and electric vehicles. However, current research on fire suppressants specifically targeting LIBs remains relatively limited. This study conducted overcharging experiments on a lithium battery module composed of two 15Ah NCM prismatic LIBs. First, the TR propagation behavior among individual battery cells in the module was experimentally analyzed. Then, the suppressive effects of C6F12O, water, and three water-in-C6F12O emulsions containing 10 %(PW1), 20 %(PM2), and 30 %(PW3) C6F12O on TR propagation were compared and evaluated. The experimental results demonstrated:(1) The TR process of overcharged LIBs can be divided into five stages: safety valve rupture, gas venting, violent combustion, stable combustion, and flame extinction.(2) A 30-second release of C6F12O, water, PW1,PW2 and PW3 all extinguished open flames within 15 s.(3) A 30-second release of these agents increased the TR propagation time between battery cells by 1.9, 2.4, 2.3, 4.2, and 3.5 times respectively compared to the control group without fire suppressants. Notably, the water-in-C6F12O emulsions with 20 % C6F12O content significantly inhibited TR propagation between battery cells, substantially extending the propagation time and providing valuable escape and rescue time for personnel. Compared to standalone C6F12O and water, the 20 % C6F12O-containing water-in-C6F12O emulsions demonstrates superior suppression of TR propagation between battery cells through synergistic effects - rapid flame extinction by C6F12O combined with efficient cooling by water. This dual-action mechanism provides a novel research direction for developing fire suppressants targeting lithium-ion battery (LIB) fires.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermal runaway
Fire extinguishing
Lithium-ion battery
Water-in- C6F12O emulsions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TR propagation behavior of lithium battery modules
Effectiveness of different fire extinguishing agents in suppressing the propagation of lithium-ion battery thermal runaway
The effectiveness of water mist and C6F12Oin suppressing the propagation of thermal runaway
Suppression effect of water-in-C6F12O emulsion fire extinguishing agents with different C6F12O contents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental battery and battery testing system.

Layout of Experimental Platform: (a) Schematic diagram of the experimental arrangement; (b) Schematic diagram of fire extinguishing agent nozzle; (c) Arrangement of Battery thermocouples at the upper end of the battery safety Valve; (d) Arrangement of thermocouples in lithium battery packs; (e) Heat flux meter.
Lithium battery module appearance: (a) E-bike lithium battery cases; (b) Lithium Battery Module; (c) Li-ion battery module overcharging method.
The thermal runaway of the lithium-ion battery pack and the suppression process of the fire extinguishing agent.
Suppression effect of water-in-C6F12O emulsion fire extinguishing agents with different C6F12O contents.
Temperature changes during the experiment: (a) PW1; (b) PW2; (c) PW3.

head.ce:title: Analysis of thermal runaway propagation behavior and suppression in overcharged ternary lithium-ion battery modules

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Fire and Emergency Rescue, China People’s Police University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thermal runaway (TR) caused by overcharging of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) often leads to fires in energy storage systems and electric vehicles. However, current research on fire suppressants specifically targeting LIBs remains relatively limited. This study conducted overcharging experiments on a lithium battery module composed of two 15Ah NCM prismatic LIBs. First, the TR propagation behavior among individual battery cells in the module was experimentally analyzed. Then, the suppressive effects of C6F12O, water, and three water-in-C6F12O emulsions containing 10 %(PW1), 20 %(PM2), and 30 %(PW3) C6F12O on TR propagation were compared and evaluated. The experimental results demonstrated:(1) The TR process of overcharged LIBs can be divided into five stages: safety valve rupture, gas venting, violent combustion, stable combustion, and flame extinction.(2) A 30-second release of C6F12O, water, PW1,PW2 and PW3 all extinguished open flames within 15 s.(3) A 30-second release of these agents increased the TR propagation time between battery cells by 1.9, 2.4, 2.3, 4.2, and 3.5 times respectively compared to the control group without fire suppressants. Notably, the water-in-C6F12O emulsions with 20 % C6F12O content significantly inhibited TR propagation between battery cells, substantially extending the propagation time and providing valuable escape and rescue time for personnel. Compared to standalone C6F12O and water, the 20 % C6F12O-containing water-in-C6F12O emulsions demonstrates superior suppression of TR propagation between battery cells through synergistic effects - rapid flame extinction by C6F12O combined with efficient cooling by water. This dual-action mechanism provides a novel research direction for developing fire suppressants targeting lithium-ion battery (LIB) fires.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their high energy density, long cycle life, and memory-free characteristics, LIBs are widely used in e-bikes [1–4]. However, individual LIBs in e-bike battery modules can experience TR under conditions such as mechanical abuse, electrical abuse, or thermal abuse [5–7]. A thermally runaway LIB releases a large amount of heat, which can trigger TR in other batteries within the module, leading to serious accidents such as fires or explosions [8,9]. Therefore, it is particularly important to study the characteristics of TR propagation in LIB modules and the measures to inhibit such propagation [10,11].

At present, many scholars have conducted research on the TR propagation behavior of LIB modules [12–14]. He et al. [15] analyzed the characteristics of surface temperature, ambient temperature, and voltage of ternary LIB packs under different charge rates, and divided the entire TR process into three stages: early, middle, and late. Based on the analysis results, they developed a three-level early warning algorithm and device for TR in ternary LIB packs. Li et al. [16] investigated the impact of heating position on the TR propagation of LIB modules in a battery box, and found that when the TR heat source is located outside the battery box, it takes longer for the first TR to occur compared to when it is located inside the battery box. However, the speed of TR propagation and the maximum battery temperature are higher when the heat source is located outside the battery box. Ouyang et al. [17] conducted a series of TR experiments on 18650 LIB modules and found that the distance between batteries, the state of charge (SOC) of the batteries, and the phase change material (PCM) encapsulating the LIB modules all have an impact on the TR propagation of 18650 LIB modules. Sun et al. [18] tested the impact of different airflow rates on the TR propagation of pouch LIBs in a dynamic pressure chamber under low-pressure environments of 20 Kpa and 95 Kpa, and found that TR propagation between LIBs can be slowed down under low ambient pressure, and TR propagation becomes faster as the airflow rate increases. Li et al. [19] studied the TR propagation characteristics of LIBs with different cathode materials and found that the risk of TR propagation increases with the nickel content in the cathode material, and the maximum temperature during thermal runaway is in the order of NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM111.

Research on suppressing thermal runaway propagation in lithium battery modules primarily focuses on lithium battery material modification, thermal management technology, fire-resistant and insulating materials, and emergency suppression. n terms of lithium battery material modification, key areas include cathode, anode, separator, and electrolyte development. For example, inspired by Ficus carica, Wang et al. [20] designed CoS₂@APFS microspheres as an anode material to enhance the electrochemical performance and thermal safety of lithium batteries, achieving a 54.2 % reduction in the maximum temperature rise rate. Thermal management technologies mainly involve liquid cooling and phase change materials (PCMs). Zhang et al. [21] investigated the thermal runaway behavior of high-specific-energy 21700 batteries under immersion conditions and proposed a novel liquid-immersion thermal regulation method, which reduced the safety distance to 3.0 mm and lowered the peak temperature of adjacent cells by nearly 60 %. Additionally, Sheng et al. [22] analyzed a honeycomb liquid cooling jacket designed for 21700 cylindrical LIBs, demonstrating that staggered flow directions in the honeycomb cooling sleeve enabled lower temperature standard deviations and more uniform thermal distribution. Chen et al. [23] evaluated packaging materials such as aerogel felts, thermal conductive gels, and dual-phase change materials, noting that thermal conductive gels and PCMs improved safety and emergency response under extreme conditions, with thermal conductive gels showing the most promise in delaying safety valve rupture and TR. Fire-resistant and insulating material research includes ceramic fiber composites, flame-retardant epoxy resin boards, and mica boards. Wang et al. [24] developed a novel phosphorus-doped MoS₂ flame-retardant epoxy resin board, where a 3 mm thickness effectively blocked TR propagation. Fire-resistant and insulating material research includes ceramic fiber composites, flame-retardant epoxy resin boards, and mica boards. Wang et al. [25] developed a novel phosphorus-doped MoS₂ flame-retardant epoxy resin board, where a 3 mm thickness effectively blocked TR propagation. In emergency suppression, studies involve water-based fire extinguishers, liquid nitrogen, and C6F12O. Huang et al. [26] examined the suppression effect of C6F12O on NCM pouch LIB TR in confined spaces, finding that at the critical fire-extinguishing dosage, C6F12O significantly inhibited TR in NCM pouch cells.

Although C6F12O can effectively inhibit TR and its propagation in LIBs, its heat of vaporization is 88.0 kJ/kg (only 4 % of that of water), requiring a large amount of C6F12O to continuously cool the thermally runaway LIB modules [27]. A small amount of C6F12O not only cannot inhibit TR in LIBs but may even exacerbate it [28]. For example, Wang et al. [29] conducted fire extinguishing efficiency experiments on 60 Ah lithium iron phosphate batteries using 10 fire extinguishing agents such as water mist, C6F12O, liquid nitrogen, and C6F12O, and found that releasing fire extinguishing agents such as water mist, C6F12O, and C6F12O for 20 s could not inhibit the propagation of TR behavior, and adjacent batteries showed signs of TR to varying degrees. In response to the problem of insufficient cooling by C6F12O, the following measures are currently available: (1) synergistic inhibition by C6F12O and water mist intermittent spraying [30]; (2) synergistic inhibition by C6F12O and liquid nitrogen or liquid carbon dioxide [31,32]; (3) preparation of C6F12O microcapsules. Although these measures solve the problem of insufficient cooling by C6F12O, they are technically and economically challenging and cannot be applied on a large scale [33].

This study conducted an overcharge experiment on a LIB module composed of two 15Ah NCM111 prismatic lithium batteries to analyze the TR propagation behavior of the LIB module and propose measures to inhibit the TR propagation of the LIB module. In order to solve the problem of insufficient cooling by C6F12O, a stable water-in- C6F12O emulsion was formed by dispersing C6F12O in water. This emulsion not only has the high efficiency and environmental benefits of C6F12O and water in inhibiting LIB fires, but also has the advantages of simple preparation and can be applied on a large scale. At the same time, experiments have proven the effectiveness of this emulsion.

In this experiment, 15Ah prismatic NCM111 LIB, which are commonly used in LIB electric bicycles, were used. As shown in Fig. 1a, the battery has dimensions of 110 mm (H) ×68 mm (L) ×19 mm (W), a mass of 305 g, and other parameters provided in Table 1. Before the experiment, each battery was subjected to three 0.5 C charge-discharge cycles using the Neware high-performance LIB testing system, and finally charged to 100 % state of charge. The batteries were allowed to rest for 24 h before the start of the experiment to ensure the stability of the internal electrochemical system of the batteries during the experiment.

The experimental platform comprises a combustion chamber, a water mist device, an overcharge device, data acquisition equipment, and other components, as shown in Fig. 2a. The combustion chamber is a 1 m × 1 m × 1 m stainless steel box with a 0.8 m circular hole on the front face to communicate with the outside world. Inside the chamber, there are water mist pipes and nozzles, a heat flux meter, lithium battery fixtures, and other equipment. The nozzle of the water mist device is installed 20 cm away from the obliquely above the battery module, as shown in Fig. 2b. It is powered by a high-pressure water pump, and the pressure and flow rate are controlled by a control cabinet. During the experiment, the working pressure is set to 3 MPa and the flow rate to 420 mL/min. The SBG01 heat flux meter has a working range of 0–30Kw/m2 and is installed 20 cm horizontally away from the battery pack, as shown in Fig. 2e. There are three armored thermocouples with a diameter of 3 mm spaced 15 cm apart, located 5 cm above the battery to collect flame temperature, as shown in Fig. 2c. One K-type thermocouple with a diameter of 1.0 mm is installed at the middle position of the upper and middle surfaces on the side where cell 1 and cell 2 are in contact, and one K-type thermocouple with a diameter of 1.0 mm is installed at the middle position of the upper and middle surfaces on both sides of cell 2, totaling six thermocouples, as shown in Fig. 2(d).

water-in- C6F12O emulsion is a stable emulsion dispersion system formed under the action of emulsifiers, with as C6F12O the dispersed phase and water as the continuous phase. It combines the high efficiency and environmental advantages of C6F12O and water in fire extinguishing. In this paper, C6F12O is used as the dispersed phase, water as the continuous phase, Fs-3100 fluorocarbon surfactant and polyethylene glycol as emulsifiers, and prepared by high-speed homogenizer. The detailed preparation process is as follows: first, deionized water, Fs-3100 fluorocarbon surfactant, and polyethylene glycol are placed in a beaker in a ratio of 95:2:3, and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 500 r/min for 5 min to ensure thorough mixing. Then, the mixture is allowed to stand for 3 h to obtain the continuous phase. Finally, the continuous phase and C6F12O dispersed phase are placed in a beaker in three ratios of 90:10, 80:20, and 70:30, and stirred with a homogenizer at 2000 r/min for 10 min to obtain three types of water-in- C6F12O emulsions with different C6F12O contents, abbreviated as PW1, PW2, and PW3 in that order.

This experiment aims to study the impact of TR of individual LIBs due to overcharging on other surrounding LIBs in the LIB pack of electric bicycles with ternary lithium batteries, as well as to test the inhibitory effects of C6F12O, water, and three types of water-in- C6F12O emulsions with different C6F12O contents on TR of overcharged LIBs and the propagation of TR. In reality, to protect LIBs from damage, the battery packs of electric bicycles are usually made of stainless steel and equipped with insulation boards around the batteries, as shown in Fig. 3a. The LIBs are tightly arranged in the stainless steel pack. If one LIB undergoes TR due to overcharging, the surrounding batteries will also quickly experience TR due to heating and compression. At the same time, due to the obstruction of the stainless steel and insulation boards, it is difficult for conventional fire extinguishing agents to suppress the TR of individual LIBs in the battery pack and prevent its spread. To simulate the real-world scenario of TR in individual LIBs within the battery pack of electric bicycles with ternary lithium batteries, this experiment uses a stainless steel fixture and 2 mm aluminum silicate ceramic fiber paper to protect the batteries, leaving only the sides and top of the individual LIBs exposed to the environment, as shown in Fig. 3b.

In order to study the suppression of TR propagation between LIB cells in the battery pack of an e-bike with a ternary LIB this experiment releases fire extinguishing agents for 30 s after cell 1 LIB is overcharged and catches fire due to TR for 2 s. The extinguishing agents were C6F12O, water, PW1, PW2, and PW3, and the detailed experimental conditions are shown in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TR propagation behavior of lithium battery modules
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the observation records and temperature data from the overcharge experiment of cells in the LIB module, the following TR behaviors in cells due to overcharge within the battery module can be observed.

As shown in Fig. 4 during the early stage of overcharge, there were no significant changes in the appearance of cell 1 and cell 2 cells in the LIB module, and their temperatures rose slowly. As the overcharge progressed, the temperature of cell 1 began to increase gradually, and its internal components started to decompose, producing gases that led to an increase in internal pressure. Due to the increased internal pressure, the safety valve of cell 1 ruptured at 821 s, releasing a small amount of white smoke. With the continuous input of electrical energy and the progression of side reactions within the battery, the white smoke released by the safety valve of cell 1 began to increase at 896 s and sprayed out in large quantities at 905 s. At this point, the temperature at position TC2 of cell 1 was 117°C, and an internal short circuit occurred. As the violent chemical reactions continued inside the battery, sparks began to appear at the safety valve of cell 1 at 906 s. The large amount of white smoke was ignited by the sparks spraying out of the safety valve, leading to a flashover at 908 s, with flames filling the entire combustion chamber. The combustible gases in the combustion chamber were significantly consumed during the intense combustion, and the reduction in gas ejected from the safety valve of cell 1 caused the flames at the safety valve to gradually diminish. By 912 s, the flames stabilized, with a height of approximately 25 cm. As the TR reaction continued, the aluminum casing of cell 1 began to melt and drip under high temperatures, exposing its internal positive and negative electrodes and electrolyte, resulting in the entire cell 1 being engulfed in flames.

As the TR of cell 1continued to heat cell 2 and the internal chemical reactions within cell 2 intensified, the safety valve of cell 2 ruptured at 946 s, releasing a large amount of white smoke. The white smoke encountered the flames from the TR of cell 1 at 947 s and ignited, but the combustion was short-lived, with the flames being extinguished by another rapid ejection of white smoke from the safety valve of cell 2 at 949 s. Subsequently, the second ejection of white smoke from the safety valve of cell 2 encountered residual flames and caused a flashover at 952 s, with intense combustion lasting for 2 s. The intense TR reaction within cell 2 caused the surface temperature of cell 2 to rise rapidly, with the temperature at position TC3 reaching 846℃ and a maximum temperature increase rate of 48℃/s. The high temperature caused the aluminum casing of cell 2 to melt, resulting in horizontal flames being ejected from the side of cell 2 at 954 s, forming a 90° angle with the flames ejected from the safety valve. The two massive flames burned intensely in the combustion chamber. As the combustible gases within cell 2 were rapidly ejected and burned from the safety valve and the side, the flames on the side of cell 2 gradually diminished and disappeared at 957 s. The flame height at the safety valve of cell 2 became approximately 25 cm at 957 s and continued to burn until 983 s. As the thermal runaway reaction ended, the flames at the safety valve of cell 2 began to decrease at 957 s and disappeared at 1026 s.

As shown in Fig. 5a, Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c, during the period from 905 s to 935 s, the surface temperature of cell 1 rose rapidly. The temperature rise rate at position TC2 of cell 1 reached a maximum of 73℃/s, and the maximum radiative heat flux of the flames above the safety valve of cell 1 reached 5.8 kW/m². After 935 s, the flames above the safety valve of cell 1 gradually disappeared, leaving only a small amount of flames on its side. At this point, the surface temperature of cell 1 reached around 550℃, and the temperatures at positions TC3 and TC4 on the surface of cell 2 also reached around 400℃. Meanwhile, the temperatures at positions TC5 and TC6 on the surface of cell 2 were around 150℃, indicating that cell 2 was about to experience TR.

As shown in Fig. 6, the TR process of LIBs can be summarized into five stages: safety valve rupture, release of white smoke, intense combustion, stable combustion, and flame extinction. Wang et al. [34]'s study on overcharge-induced TR behavior in lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries revealed that this process typically involves characteristic phenomena such as safety valve rupture, gas release, combustion, and ejection. This indicates that ternary LIBs exhibit similar thermal runaway phenomena during overcharge compared to LFP batteries.

Stage I: As current continuously flows into LIB cells, their internal resistance gradually increases, leading to significant Joule heat generation. When the rate of Joule heat production exceeds the heat dissipation rate of the battery cells, thermal energy progressively accumulates within the LIBs [35,36]. When the accumulated heat raises the temperature of the lithium-ion battery cell to 90℃, the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) decomposes, producing C2H4, CO2, and a significant amount of heat, which increases the internal pressure of the battery and causes the safety valve to rupture. The reaction formula is shown in Eq. 1.
(1)
LiEDC
→
80 − 120 ℃
L
i
2
C
O
3
+
C
2
H
4
↑ + C
O
2
↑

Stage II: When the heat generated by Joule heating from the current and the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) in the lithium-ion battery cell raises the temperature to around 120°C, the polyethylene (PE) or polypropylene (PP) separator in the LIB begins to melt, causing a short circuit between the positive and negative electrodes. This short circuit leads to the decomposition of the electrolyte, producing a large amount of combustible gases. These gases carry the electrolyte and broken graphite anode particles and other internal substances of the LIB out through the safety valve, forming white smoke that fills the combustion chamber [37,38]. The reaction equations are shown in Eq. 2 and Eq. 3.
(2)
C
3
H
4
O
3
( EC ) → C
O
2
↑ +
C
2
H
4
↑ +
H
2
O
(3)
C
3
H
6
O
3
( DMC ) → C
O
2
↑ + C
H
3
OH + C
H
4
↑

Stage III: Sparks generated by a short circuit between the positive and negative electrodes inside the LIB, or by the friction between a large amount of substances rapidly ejected from the safety valve and the valve opening, ignite the flammable gases in the combustion chamber, causing a flashover to occur instantly. After the flashover, the flammable gases ejected from the safety valve of the LIB begin to burn intensely [39,40].

Stage IV: As the flammable gases in the combustion chamber are consumed by combustion and the ejection of flammable gases from the safety valve of the LIB decreases, the flames at the safety valve of the cell begin to burn steadily. This process is mainly the stable combustion of the electrolyte as fuel, providing heat for the propagation of TR in the LIB module. The reaction formulas are shown in Eq. 4 and Eq. 5.
(4)
C
4
H
4
O
3
EC
+ 3
O
2
→ 3 C
O
2
↑ + 2
H
2
O
(5)
C
4
H
6
O
3
DMC
+ 3
O
2
→ 3 C
O
2
↑ + 3
H
2
O
Stage V: As the SEI film, separator, electrolyte, and other components inside the LIB decompose and burn out, the flames at the safety valve gradually become smaller and eventually disappear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of different fire extinguishing agents in suppressing the propagation of lithium-ion battery thermal runaway
The effectiveness of water mist and C6F12Oin suppressing the propagation of thermal runaway
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments 2 and 3 tested the suppression effects of fine water mist and C6F12O on TR and its propagation in overcharged lithium battery modules. In the experiment，the fire extinguishing agent was released for 30 s starting 2 s after cell1 exhibited flame ejection during overcharge-induced TR. Fig. 7 illustrates the TR process of the battery module and the suppression by fire extinguishing agent，with the flame ejection from cell1 during overcharge TR marked as 0 s. The subsequent stages include: agent release at 2 s, changes at 15 s after agent release, changes at 30 s after agent release, initial TR of cell2, severe TR of cell2, and final termination of TR in the battery module.

From Fig. 7, it can be observed that when the fire extinguishing agent is released at 2 s, the fire suppression effects of C6F12O and water mist in the confined space are different due to their different fire suppression mechanisms. When C6F12O comes into contact with the jet flame of a LIB, its low boiling point and low heat of vaporization cause it to rapidly vaporize into C6F12O gas upon release. A large amount of C6F12O gas quickly extinguishes the jet flame from the safety valve of the cell 1 through cooling, chemical suppression, and isolation of air. When water mist comes into contact with the flame, the high-speed movement of small droplets blows the flame downward towards the bottom of the platform. As can be seen from Fig. 7, the top of the flame at the safety valve of the cell1 has been blown into contact with the bottom platform by the high-speed movement of small droplets. Although both C6F12O and water mist can basically extinguish the ejected flame at the safety valve of the cell1 within 15 s, during the suppression process of water mist, electric sparks can be observed at the safety valve opening of the cell1, leading to the observation of a small reignition flame at the safety valve of the cell1 after the release of water mist is stopped. Compared with the experimental group without fire extinguishing agent suppression, the C6F12O experimental group and the water mist experimental group delayed the TR of the cell2 by 36 s and 55 s respectively, and there was no open flame throughout the TR process of the cell2. This indicates that both C6F12O and water mist have a certain inhibitory effect on the TR of LIBs, but water mist may cause short circuits and generate electric sparks [41].

As can be seen from Fig. 8a, when water mist is released, although the temperature rise on the inner surface of the cell1 in the water mist experiment is suppressed and even slightly decreased, the outer surface temperature of the cell2 rises from 50℃ to around 180℃, which in turn triggers the TR of cell2. There are three main factors that prevent water mist from reducing the internal temperature of the LIB module and inhibiting the propagation of TR between LIBs. First, after release, the water mist first suppresses the flame ejected from the safety valve of the LIB, and the tiny droplets cannot penetrate the flame to reach the battery module to cool it down, resulting in most of the water mist being consumed in suppressing the flame [42]. Second, when the water mist directly cools down the LIB after extinguishing the flame, the small droplets of water mist form a layer of steam on the surface of the LIB when they come into contact with the high-temperature surface of the LIB, preventing the small droplets of water mist from continuously cooling the surface of the LIB [43]. Finally, in the LIB module, the two cells are tightly fixed together by a stainless steel fixture, leaving no gap between the two cells. Moreover, there is a 2 mm aluminum silicate ceramic fiber paper between the cells and the stainless steel fixture, which enables the water mist to only cool the smaller side surfaces and top surfaces of the cells. Sheng et al. [44] conducted an experimental and numerical study on the anisotropic thermal conductivity of LIBs. They pointed out that the thermal conductivity of LIBs exhibits strong anisotropy, and the temperature on the minor lateral surfaces is relatively low. Therefore, using water mist to cool the entire battery through these minor lateral surfaces has limited effectiveness. As can be observed from Fig. 8b, after the release of C6F12O, the temperature of the LIB pack with TR hardly drops. However, compared with the control group without fire extinguishing agent release, the heating rate of the cells in this experimental group slows down. There are two main reasons for this phenomenon in the C6F12O experimental group. First, the low boiling point of C6F12O causes its small droplets to vaporize before they even come into contact with the high-temperature battery surface, making it impossible to directly cool the high-temperature LIBs. Second, the amount of C6F12O released is relatively small, resulting in the heat absorbed by its vaporization being less than the heat released by the TR of the LIBs. However, C6F12O still absorbs a large amount of the heat from the TR of the LIBs, reducing the heating rate of the LIB pack and having a certain inhibitory effect on the propagation of thermal runaway in the LIB pack.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppression effect of water-in-C6F12O emulsion fire extinguishing agents with different C6F12O contents
ce:section.ce:para: The suppression effects of PW1, PW2, and PW3 on the TR of LIBs caused by overcharge and the spread of TR between LIBs in a LIB pack were tested in experiments 4, 5, and 6. During the experiments, the fire extinguishing agent was released for 30 s starting 2 s after cell1 exhibited flame ejection due to TR caused by overcharge. Fig. 9 illustrates the suppression process of water-in- C6F12O emulsion fire extinguishing agents with different C6F12O contents on the TR of the LIB pack and the spread of TR. The moment when the cell1 ejected flames due to thermal runaway was recorded as 0 s, followed by the release of the fire extinguishing agent at 2 s, the change at 15 s after the release of the fire extinguishing agent, the change at 30 s after the release of the fire extinguishing agent, the onset of TR of cell 2, the intense TR of cell 2, and the end of TR.

As can be observed from Fig. 9, water-in-C6F12O emulsions with different C6F12O contents have varying suppression effects on the ejection of flames from the safety valve of a LIB during TR caused by overcharge. When PW1 comes into contact with the flames, it exhibits a phenomenon similar to that of water mist, with high-speed PW1 droplets blowing the flames towards the bottom of the experimental platform. This phenomenon occurs because the C6F12O content in PW1 is not high, so there is not enough vaporized C6F12O gas to cool down and chemically suppress the flame. As a result, the fire extinguishing mechanism of PW1 is similar to that of pure water mist, which can only first interfere with the flame through the high-speed movement of small droplets, and then gradually absorb heat through water vaporization to produce water vapor for cooling and isolating air to suffocate and extinguish the flame. When PW2 comes into contact with the flame, it can be observed that the flame is blown by the high-speed movement of PW2 droplets to a horizontal position 5 cm away from the upper end of the LIB. This is due to the fact that the C6F12O in the small droplets of PW2 vaporizes, expands, and ruptures upon heating, causing the small water droplets to break into even smaller droplets and accelerating the vaporization of PW2 droplets. As a result, PW2 does not have the same impact on the flame as PW1. However, a large amount of water vapor and C6F12O gas quickly fills the combustion chamber, extinguishing the flame through heat absorption and cooling, air isolation and suffocation, as well as chemical suppression [45]. When PW3 comes into contact with the flame, it can be clearly observed that the flame is less impacted by the PW3 droplets, only slightly tilting to one side. However, the upper part of the ejected flame is quickly extinguished. The main reason for this phenomenon is that a large number of C6F12O droplets in the small droplets of PW3 expand and rupture upon heating, causing the small water droplets to break into even smaller droplets. These smaller droplets are vaporized by the heat radiation of the flame before they come into contact with it, so they have a smaller impact on the flame. At the same time, a large amount of water vapor and C6F12O gas fills the upper part of the combustion chamber, extinguishing the upper part of the flame through heat absorption and cooling, air isolation and suffocation, as well as chemical suppression. Finally, as shown in Fig. 9, compared to the experiment without fire extinguishing agent suppression, PW1, PW2, and PW3 delayed the TR of cell 2 by 53 s, 127 s, and 98 s, respectively. Furthermore, no open flames occurred during the entire TR process of cell 2 in all three groups. These results indicate that the three water-in- C6F12O emulsions with different C6F12O contents have a certain inhibitory effect on the spread of TR in LIB packs, with PW2 exhibiting the most pronounced suppression effect.

As shown in Fig. 10, after the release of the three water-in-C6F12O emulsions with different C6F12O contents, the temperatures at positions TC1, TC2, TC3, and TC4 on the surface of cell1 and cell2 in the battery pack decreased to varying degrees. However, the temperatures at positions TC5 and TC6 on the outer surface of cell2 slowly rose from around 40℃ to around 180℃ before TR occurred. After the release of the fire extinguishing agents, they were unable to directly cool the inner surfaces of cell1 and cell2, and could only cool their side and upper surfaces. At the same time, when the extinguishing agents were released, the severe TR of cell1 resulted in the heat released by the TR being greater than the heat absorbed by the cooling effect of the extinguishing agents. However, as the extinguishing agents continued to be released and the TR was gradually suppressed, the heat absorbed by the cooling effect of the extinguishing agents gradually became greater than the heat released by the TR of cell1. As shown in Fig. 10, after the release of the three water-in-C6F12O emulsions with different C6F12O contents, the temperatures at positions TC1, TC2, TC3, and TC4 on the surface of cell1 and cell2 in the battery pack decreased to varying degrees. However, the temperatures at positions TC5 and TC6 on the outer surface of cell2 slowly rose from around 40℃ to around 180℃ before TR occurred. After the release of the fire extinguishing agents, they were unable to directly cool the inner surfaces of cell1 and cell2, and could only cool their side and upper surfaces. At the same time, when the extinguishing agents were released, the severe TR of cell1 resulted in the heat released by the TR being greater than the heat absorbed by the cooling effect of the extinguishing agents. However, as the extinguishing agents continued to be released and the TR was gradually suppressed, the heat absorbed by the cooling effect of the extinguishing agents gradually became greater than the heat released by the TR of cell1. This caused the temperatures at position TC2 on the surface of cell1 and position TC4 on the surface of cell2 in the three experimental groups to first slowly rise and then slowly fall. However, the magnitude and duration of the temperature decrease at position TC2 on the surface of cell1 and position TC4 on the surface of cell2 varied among the three experimental groups. Among them, the temperature decrease at position TC2 on the surface of cell1 and position TC4 on the surface of cell2 in the PW2 experimental group was the most significant and sustained for the longest duration. As shown in Fig. 10b, the temperature at position TC2 on the surface of cell1 decreased from 596℃ to 381℃over 150 s. This was followed by the PW3 experimental group, where the temperature at position TC2 on the surface of cell1 decreased from 551℃to 453℃over 120 s, as shown in Fig. 10c. Finally, in the PW1 experimental group, the temperature at position TC2 on the surface of cell1 decreased from 616℃to 551℃over 65 s, as shown in Fig. 10a.

In this study, overcharge experiments were conducted using a LIB module composed of two 15Ah NCM prismatic lithium batteries to analyze the TR propagation behavior of the module. Additionally, the inhibitory effects of C6F12O, water, and three water-in- C6F12O emulsions with C6F12O contents of 10 %, 20 %, and 30 % on the TR of the LIB module were compared and evaluated. The following conclusions were drawn from this study:
1.
The TR process of overcharged aluminum prismatic lithium batteries can be summarized into five stages: safety valve rupture, release of white smoke, intense combustion, stable combustion, and flame extinction. In practice, lithium-ion battery cells are tightly packed in battery boxes with stainless steel enclosures and insulation plates, which makes it difficult for fire extinguishing agents such as water mist and C6F12O to suppress TR in individual battery cells and prevent its propagation. Therefore, when fighting fires in LIB modules, it is necessary to first open the external protective casing of the battery module to expose the individual battery cells to the extinguishing agents, which can significantly improve the fire extinguishing efficiency.
2.
Both C6F12O, water, and three types of water-in- C6F12O emulsions with C6F12O concentrations of 10 %, 20 %, and 30 % were able to extinguish thermal runaway fires in 15Ah LIB modules within 15 s without re-ignition. A 30-second release of these agents increased the TR propagation time between battery cells by 1.9, 2.4, 2.3, 4.2, and 3.5 times respectively compared to the control group without fire suppressants. Notably, the water-in- C6F12O emulsions with 20 % C6F12O content significantly inhibited TR propagation between battery cells, substantially extending the propagation time and providing valuable escape and rescue time for personnel.
3.
In water-in- C6F12O emulsions, water and C6F12O exhibit synergistic and mutually reinforcing effects in suppressing LIB TR fires, demonstrating better suppression performance than single fire extinguishing agents. C6F12O rapidly extinguishes flames and accelerates the endothermic vaporization of water, while water absorbs a large amount of heat to cool the LIBs and prevent the premature volatilization of C6F12O.

Kong Lingrui: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Zhang Tianwei: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Liu Hao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhang Xiaorui: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Su Gengyi: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Tian Jikai: Writing – original draft, Methodology.

Financial support by Science and Technology Program of Guangdong Province (2023A1111120002), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2023507001), Key R&D Program of Hebei Province (22375417D), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51804314).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2023507001) for help.
